When the Pandyas Thanked the Kakatiyas: A 14th-Century Inscription from Vriddhachalam

# When the Pandyas Thanked the Kakatiyas: A 14th-Century Inscription from Vriddhachalam

[](https://substack.com/@krishnants)

![Krishnan's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!CoKq!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fc27f14d3-b199-4948-93ad-713cb7d3098e_398x398.jpeg)

[Krishnan](https://substack.com/@krishnants)

Jun 06, 2026

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Share

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!MbxA!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Ff1caf548-4ec4-4654-a861-2d713a8042a5_1023x764.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!MbxA!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Ff1caf548-4ec4-4654-a861-2d713a8042a5_1023x764.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

An inscription at the Vriddhagireeswarar Temple in Vriddhachalam reveals interesting details about the changing political landscape of Tamilakam during the 14th century. Before going into the inscription, it is important to understand the background.

In the early 14th century, Maravarman Kulasekara Pandyan was ruling from Madurai. The Pandya kingdom was then one of the wealthiest powers in South India. The Italian traveller Marco Polo, who visited the region in the late 13th century, speaks in glowing terms about the prosperity of the Pandya country, its pearls, trade, and royal wealth. The kingdom that had reached such heights of power and prosperity soon plunged into a disastrous civil war.

Kulasekara Pandyan had two sons, Sundara Pandya and Veera Pandya. Though Sundara Pandya was the elder, Kulasekara is said to have favoured Veera Pandya, considering him more suitable to rule. This naturally created deep resentment. According to later accounts, Sundara Pandya killed his father and seized the throne. However, Veera Pandya, who enjoyed the support of many officials and ministers, soon marched with an army, defeated Sundara Pandya, and captured Madurai.

Left without strong support within the kingdom, Sundara Pandya looked outside for help. This internal weakness came at the worst possible time. Alauddin Khalji of Delhi had already heard of the immense wealth of the Pandya country, known in the Persian chronicles as Ma‘bar, and had sent his general Malik Kafur on a southern expedition. Malik Kafur had already subdued the Hoysalas and reached Dwarasamudra, the Hoysala capital. Sundara Pandya went to Dwarasamurdra and sought the support of Malik Kafur to defeat Veera Pandya and restore the kingdom to him. Seizing the opportunity, Malik Kafur happily entered Tamilakam during 1311 CE, raided several places, and carried out widespread plunder. Contemporary and later accounts associate this campaign with attacks on major temple centres, including Chidambaram and Madurai. Malik Kafur eventually entered Madurai, looted the region, and returned to Delhi with enormous wealth, including elephants, horses, gold, and precious stones.

After Malik Kafur returned to Delhi with the enormous wealth looted from the Pandya country, Veera Pandya seems to have emerged again from hiding and attempted to re-establish his authority. However, instead of learning from the disaster that had already befallen the kingdom, Sundara Pandya once again resumed his struggle against him. The civil war between the Pandya brothers continued, further weakening the kingdom at a time when Tamilakam was already shaken by the northern invasion.

It was at this moment that the Chera ruler Ravivarman Kulasekhara entered the political scene. Taking advantage of the disturbed situation in the Pandya country, he marched into Pandya Nadu and asserted his power over the region. His inscriptions claim that he defeated Veera Pandya and also Sundara Pandya, the brother of Veera Pandya. He then extended his authority further and reached Srirangam. One of his inscriptions at Srirangam is also important for another reason, as it records the celebration of Deepavali in the temple.

Ravivarman Kulasekhara did not stop with Pandya Nadu. Continuing his victorious march, he advanced northwards and brought large parts of the Tamil country, including Kanchipuram, under his influence. His control over Kanchi and the northern Tamil region appears to have lasted only for a short period, roughly between 1313 and 1315 CE. For a brief moment, he projected himself as a ruler over the Chera, Chola and Pandya regions, and his inscriptions reflect this imperial ambition.

Unable to withstand the Chera advance, the Pandya brothers finally reconciled with each other. They sought help from the powerful Kakatiya ruler Prataparudradeva of Warangal. Prataparudra responded by sending his general Muppidi Nayaka with an army. According to the Kanchipuram inscription, Muppidi Nayaka marched south and drove out the forces that had occupied Thondaimandalam around 1316 CE. Fierce fighting followed, and the Chera forces were eventually pushed back. Ravivarman Kulasekhara’s brief hold over the northern Tamil country came to an end, and Pandya authority was restored, though the kingdom itself had already been deeply weakened by civil war, invasion and external intervention.

It is in this larger context that the inscription at Vriddhachalam becomes extremely important. The inscription belongs to *Konerinmaikandan Sundara Pandya*, which suggests that Sundara Pandya had finally managed to establish himself as ruler after the Chera forces were driven out. The inscription begins with an introductory verse in Sanskrit in praise of Mupidi Kshamapati, king of Vikrama Pattana.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!y04Z!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F154e13b5-218b-446a-b7ef-8c04243a11f7_1600x1174.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!y04Z!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F154e13b5-218b-446a-b7ef-8c04243a11f7_1600x1174.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

One of the most striking features of the inscription is the manner in which it refers to the Kakatiya ruler Prataparudradeva. Sundara Pandya calls him his elder brother (*Nam Thamayanar*), showing the deep gratitude that the Pandya side felt towards the Kakatiya king. This was not a casual expression. It reflected the crucial role played by Prataparudra in helping the Pandyas recover from the Chera occupation. The inscription then goes on to record services instituted in the name of Muppidi Nayaka, the Kakatiya general who had led the campaign and emerged as the saviour of the Pandya kingdom.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!i3I7!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F9d4d68e3-d404-4eee-8309-8ffcc0447f9f_1600x1378.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!i3I7!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F9d4d68e3-d404-4eee-8309-8ffcc0447f9f_1600x1378.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The inscription records a grant made to the Vriddhagireeswarar temple. It mentions the assignment of taxes from eight villages, amounting to two thousand pieces of gold, given as devadana iraiyili to the temple. The taxes included kadamai, antarayam, kariyavaratchi, ponvari, kasukadamai and mavadai. These were various categories of dues and fiscal obligations assigned to the villages.

What is particularly interesting is that the land revenue itself does not appear to have been exempted. This is a revealing detail. In a more prosperous and stable period, temple grants often involved wider tax exemptions. Here, however, only certain categories of taxes were assigned, while the basic land revenue seems to have remained intact. This may reflect the strained economic condition of the country after years of civil war, invasion, temple plunder and external intervention. The kingdom had survived, but its financial strength had clearly been weakened.

The purpose of the grant was to support various temple services. These included offerings, worship, ceremonial anointment of the deity, the burning of twenty lamps, and repairs and renovations to the temple. All these services were instituted in the name of Muppidi Nayaka, the pradhani of Prataparudradeva. This makes the inscription historically remarkable. A Pandya king, ruling from Tamilakam, records a temple endowment in honour of a Kakatiya general from Warangal, whose military intervention had helped restore Pandya authority.

Thus, the Vriddhachalam inscription captures the turbulent political reality of the early 14th century. It shows a Pandya kingdom weakened by internal conflict, a Chera ruler who briefly expanded into the Tamil country, a Kakatiya monarch who intervened from Warangal, and a temple that became the medium through which political gratitude was publicly recorded. More than a mere grant, this inscription stands as evidence of how deeply interconnected the political world of South India had become during this period.

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

18
